name: Master of Architecture

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 20 referenced courses form a clear professional M.Arch spine: a six-step studio sequence (Studio I-IV plus Advanced Studios I-II, deepestPrereqChain 5), paralleled by two-course sequences in History, Construction, Environmental Control Systems, and Structures that feed the studios, capped by a Synthesis Seminar and Professional Practice I-II. Progression is well-ordered and the technical courses are timed to support studio work; the main weakness is that no plan-of-study groups were parsed (requirementGroups 0), so term-by-term balance and elective load are not visible.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit term-by-term plan of study mapping each studio to its co-requisite technical courses so the coordinated sequencing that clearly exists in the prerequisites is legible on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy: missingCourseCount is 0 (no dangling references), and the studio chain reaches a sensible depth of 5 (501G to 502G to 503G to 504G to 505G to 506G) with technical prerequisites like Structures I to II and History I to II well-formed. The only blemish is ARCH 542G, whose prerequisiteRaw string has course-catalog description text bled into it, muddying an otherwise clean set.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the ARCH 542G prerequisite field so it reads only 'ARCH 541G' and audit for other cases where description text has contaminated the prerequisite parse.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the data, which is a real gap for a first-professional, NAAB-accredited degree where accreditation is organized around explicit Student Performance / Program Criteria.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the NAAB Program Criteria the degree satisfies as stated learning outcomes and map each to the studios and technical courses that assess them.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null and creditReconcileDelta cannot be computed because no plan groups were parsed, so a prospective student cannot see the total credit load or how it splits among studio, technical, history, and professional-practice courses. For a full professional degree (typically ~90-110 credits, or ~60 for advanced standing) this credit opacity is a substantive transparency defect rather than a minor note.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits and the credit distribution across studio, building-technology, history/theory, and professional-practice components, and note any advanced-standing pathway.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present, specific, and informative for all 20 sampled courses, and titles clearly signal the sequence. However the page lacks a legible plan of study, a stated total credit count, and any labeled terms, so the reader must infer structure from prerequisites alone.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a structured plan-of-study table with labeled terms and a credit summary to complement the strong course descriptions.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum reflects solid contemporary professional emphases: sustainable design and passive/active environmental systems (ARCH 541G-544G), structures, landscape and urbanism, and two professional-practice courses covering legal/ethical obligations. It is comparatively light on computational design and BIM beyond the introductory Tools and Techniques courses, and shows no explicit digital-fabrication or building-performance-simulation content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or elevate a required BIM/computational-design course and a building-energy-modeling course so graduates enter practice fluent in the digital workflows firms now expect.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are substantive and specific, articulating each course's scope (e.g., ECS I on passive systems for thermal/visual comfort, Structures I on gravity and lateral behavior) rather than placeholder text; excerpts are truncated but clearly full entries exist.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated as explicit course codes with sensible sequencing and no dangling references (missingCourseCount 0); clarity is reduced only by ARCH 542G's prerequisite string absorbing description prose and by 'graduate level standing' being used where a specific gate might be clearer.

missingTopics.rationale: Beyond the introductory ARCH 500G/555G Tools and Techniques, there is no required course in Revit/BIM coordination or parametric modeling, now baseline skills for architectural practice and integrated project delivery.
ECS I-II teach principles of environmental systems but no course covers quantitative energy/daylight modeling (e.g., EnergyPlus, Ladybug) needed to hit code and net-zero targets.

Construction I-II and the Synthesis Seminar address assemblies conceptually, but there is no CNC/robotic fabrication or advanced construction-technology course connecting design to modern manufacturing.
The sequence ends at Advanced Studio II and a Synthesis Seminar with no visible research-methods or independent thesis vehicle, limiting scholarly and evidence-based design preparation.
With most carbon savings now coming from retrofitting rather than new construction, the absence of a dedicated adaptive-reuse/renovation studio or seminar is a notable gap.

emergingTrends.rationale: Embodied-carbon accounting and mass-timber/CLT construction are reshaping structural and material choices; Structures and Construction courses should incorporate life-cycle carbon and engineered-timber content.
Generative and AI-assisted design tools are entering studio and documentation workflows; the Tools and Techniques sequence should be modernized to address them critically.
Sea-level rise and extreme heat make resilient, passive-survivability design central; the ECS and studio sequence should foreground climate-adaptive strategies.
Post-pandemic emphasis on indoor environmental quality and occupant wellbeing (WELL, biophilia) is a growing client demand that the environmental-systems track could integrate.
Professional practice is increasingly measured on inclusive, community-engaged process; Professional Practice I-II could add participatory design and social-equity content.

topSuggestions: Publish the total credit count and a term-by-term plan of study showing studio/technical/history/practice distribution, replacing the current null statedTotalCredits.
State NAAB-aligned program learning outcomes and map them to the studio and technical sequence.
Add required BIM/computational-design and building-energy-modeling courses to close the biggest currency gap versus practice.
Introduce a design-research-methods course and thesis or capstone research vehicle after Advanced Studio II.
Integrate embodied-carbon, mass-timber, and adaptive-reuse content into the Structures, Construction, and studio sequences.
